title: Types of Learning — Frontier: In-Context Learning and Emergent Abilities
key_insight: Foundation models have introduced a fourth learning paradigm: in-context learning, where the model adapts to tasks at inference time without any weight updates.
body:
## In-Context Learning: A New Paradigm

```python
# Traditional learning: requires weight updates (training)
# In-context learning: model adapts from examples IN the prompt

# Zero-shot: no examples, just instructions
prompt = 'Classify sentiment (positive/negative): I loved this movie'

# Few-shot: 2-4 examples in the prompt, model infers the pattern
prompt = '''
Classify sentiment:
Text: The food was amazing. Sentiment: positive
Text: Worst service ever. Sentiment: negative
Text: The movie was okay. Sentiment:
'''
# Model outputs: positive (inferred from pattern, no training)
```

This emerged unpredictably with scale. Models under ~7B parameters don't do it reliably. Models over 65B do it very well.

## Why Does In-Context Learning Work? (Open Question)

Several competing hypotheses:

```
Hypothesis 1: Bayesian inference
  The model is implicitly computing P(task | examples)
  and performing MAP inference

Hypothesis 2: Gradient descent in the residual stream
  Olsson et al. (2022): induction heads in transformers implement
  a mechanism equivalent to gradient descent on the context

Hypothesis 3: Task retrieval
  The model has seen similar task formats in training
  and is retrieving (not adapting to) the task structure

No consensus. All three may be partially true.
```

## Chain-of-Thought: Teaching Reasoning

```python
# Standard prompting
prompt = 'What is 48 × 37?'  # Model often fails

# Chain-of-thought prompting (Wei et al., 2022)
prompt = '''What is 48 × 37?
Think step by step:
1. 40 × 37 = 1480
2. 8 × 37 = 296
3. 1480 + 296 = 1776
Answer: 1776

What is 63 × 28?
Think step by step:
'''  # Model fills in the reasoning chain automatically
```

CoT emerged as a capability only above ~100B parameters. It's a form of in-context learning of reasoning patterns.

## Implications for the Taxonomy

The traditional supervised/unsupervised/RL taxonomy is insufficient for understanding modern AI:

```
Pre-training     = self-supervised learning on scale
Instruction FT   = supervised learning on demonstrations
RLHF             = reinforcement learning from preferences
In-context       = inference-time adaptation (no learning)
Prompt optimise  = discrete search over prompt space
Fine-tuning (LoRA) = parameter-efficient supervised learning
```

Practical engineers need fluency in ALL of these — when to use which is an open engineering question with no textbook answer yet.
common_mistakes: Using few-shot prompting as a substitute for fine-tuning at scale — fine-tuning almost always outperforms prompting on domain-specific tasks
Assuming chain-of-thought works for all models — it's a large-model emergent ability, doesn't work reliably sub-30B
Treating in-context learning as free — long prompts cost more tokens, have higher latency, and don't persist across requests
mini_exercise: Read 'Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models' (Wei et al., 2022). What tasks improved most with chain-of-thought? What did NOT improve? What model size threshold did they identify?
